data.title: State Street's Q3 Earnings, Revenue Rise
Novartis receives positive CHMP opinion for Scemblix in leukemia
data.description:
Novartis announces that the Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP) of the European Medicines Agency has issued a positive opinion recommending the marketing authorization of Scemblix (asciminib) for the treatment of adults with chronic phase chronic myeloid leukemia (CML), including newly diagnosed patients.

This extension of the indication would quadruple the number of eligible patients in Europe. Results from the Phase III ASC4FIRST trial showed Scemblix to be more effective than tyrosine kinase inhibitors (imatinib, nilotinib, dasatinib, bosutinib), with fewer adverse events leading to treatment discontinuation.

Novartis says that this decision is a major step forward in the group's strategy to establish a new standard of care in Europe for CML, combining enhanced efficacy with improved tolerability.

data.title: Boston Scientific to Buy Rest of Nalu Medical for $533 Million
data.description:
By Colin Kellaher


Boston Scientific has struck a deal to buy Nalu Medical, a privately held medical-technology company focused on developing and commercializing minimally invasive solutions for chronic pain.

Boston Scientific, which initially invested in Nalu in 2017, on Friday said it will pay $533 million for the rest of the firm in a deal that will expand the Marlborough, Mass., medical-technology company's neuromodulation offerings for people living with chronic pain.

The deal is slated to close in the first half of 2026.

Boston Scientific said Nalu's neurostimulation system, which won U.S. Food and Drug Administration approval in 2019, is designed to deliver targeted relief for adults with severe, intractable chronic pain of peripheral-nerve origin, including areas such as the shoulder, lower back and knee.

Nalu expects to generate sales of more than $60 million this year, Boston Scientific said.


Write to Colin Kellaher at colin.kellaher@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

10-17-25 0743ET

CraftBank, Inc. announced that it has received ¥2.2 billion in funding from Angel Bridge Co., Ltd., Delight Ventures, Inc., JIC Venture Growth Investments Co., Ltd., Mitsubishi UFJ Capital Co., Ltd., Sumitomo Mitsui Trust Investment Co., Ltd., Japan Post Investment Corporation and another investor
data.description:
CraftBank, Inc. announced that it has received ¥2.2 billion in round of funding co-led by new investor JIC Venture Growth Investments Co., Ltd and returning investors Angel Bridge Co., Ltd. on October 17, 2025. The transaction included the participation from new investors JPS Growth Investment Limited Liability Partnership, a fund co-managed by Sumitomo Mitsui Trust Investment Co., Ltd. and Japan Post Investment Corporation, Dai-ichi Life Holdings, Inc., returning investors Delight Ventures, Inc., Mitsubishi UFJ Capital Co., Ltd., individual investor Junichi Shiroshita. The company issued the shares ¥2.2 billion through third-party allotment method and ¥1.6 billion from secondary transactions.

The company has raised ¥3.8 billion as funding till the date.

data.title: Meta Nearing $30 Billion Financing Package for Louisiana Data Center Site
FDA Approves Expanded Pediatric Indications for YUFLYMA®? (Adalimumab-Aaty) and Unbranded Adalimumab-AatY in the United States
data.description:
Celltrion, Inc. announced that the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has approved expanded indications for YUFLYMA®? (adalimumab-aaty) and its unbranded version, to include the treatment of hidradenitis suppurativa (HS) in adolescent patients aged 12 years and older, and uveitis (UV) in pediatric patients aged 2 years and older. The FDA previously approved YUFLYMA as a biosimilar to Humira®?

for a variety of indications, including rheumatoid arthritis (RA), psoriatic arthritis (PsA), juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA), ankylosing spondylitis (AS), Crohn's disease (CD), ulcerative colitis (UC), and plaque psoriasis (Ps). YUFLYMA was first introduced in the U.S. market in July 2023 and is currently available as 20mg, 40mg, and 80mg solution for injection in prefilled syringes and as 40mg and 80mg solution for injections in autoinjectors. Celltrion offers adalimumab-aaty in both branded and unbranded versions, with two pricing options to meet different patient needs and improve patient affordability.

YUFLYMA is FDA approved for the treatment of patients with rheumatoid arthritis, juvenile idiopathic arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, ankylosing spondyitis, Crohn's disease, ulcerative colitis, plaque psoriasis, hidradenitis suppurativ and uveitis. YUFLYMA has been designated by the FDA as an interchangeable biosimilar in a prefilled syringe and autoinjector. In controlled portions of clinical trials of some adalimumab products, more cases of malignancies have been observed compared to control-treated adult patients.

NMSC was reported during clinical trials for patients treated with adalimumab products. During the controlled portions of 39 global adalimumab clinical trials in adult patients with RA, PsA, AS, CD, UC, PS,HS and UV, the rate (95% confidence interval) of NMSC was 0.8 (0.52, 1.09) per 100 patient-years among control-treated patients and 0.2 (0.10, 0.59) per 100 patient-years amongst control-treated patients. In clinical trials of some adalum products, there was an approximately threefold higher rate of lymphoma than expected in the general U.S. population.

NEUROLOGIC REACTIONS: Use of TNF blocking agents, including adalimumab products, has been associated with rare cases of new onset or exacerbation of clinical symptoms and/or radiographic evidence of central nervous system demyelinating disease, including Guillain-Barre syndrome. Treatment with adalimumab products may result in the formation of autoantibodies and, rarely, in the development of a lupus-like syndrome. The most common adverse reactions in adalimumab clinical trials (>10%) were infections (e.g., upper respiratory, sinusitis), injection site reactions, headache, and rash.

Globally, prescribing information varies; refer to the individual country product label for complete information. Celltrion's FDA's FDA previously approved YUFLyMA as a biosimilar toHumira®? for a variety the company's drug-based treatment of the company's drug-based drug-based treatment of rheumatoid arthritis, psoriatic arthritis (Ps A), adult patients with RA, psoriatic arthritis, psoriatic arthritis.

The company has been designated by the FDA As an interchangeable biosimilar in a prebranded version, and the company's biosimilars immunology portfolio in the U.S., advancing affordable treatment options for patients living with chronic immune-mediated diseases.

360 One Wam Limited Reports Earnings Results for the Second Quarter and Six Months Ended September 30, 2025
data.description:
360 One Wam Limited reported earnings results for the second quarter and six months ended September 30, 2025. For the second quarter, the company reported revenue was INR 11,075.2 million compared to INR 8,928.2 million a year ago. Net income was INR 3,154.8 million compared to INR 2,454.9 million a year ago. Basic earnings per share from continuing operations was INR 7.8 compared to INR 6.75 a year ago. Diluted earnings per share from continuing operations was INR 7.56 compared to INR 6.47 a year ago.
For the six months, revenue was INR 20,875.6 million compared to INR 18,283.6 million a year ago. Net income was INR 6,001.5 million compared to INR 4,892.3 million a year ago. Basic earnings per share from continuing operations was INR 14.96 compared to INR 13.51 a year ago. Diluted earnings per share from continuing operations was INR 14.52 compared to INR 12.95 a year ago.

Verisure Suffers Data Breach According to SVT - Shares Drop
data.description:
Verisure has experienced a data breach, according to reports from SVT. The company's shares have fallen in response to the news.

Further details regarding the extent of the breach or the nature of the compromised data have not yet been disclosed. The incident has raised concerns among investors, leading to a noticeable decline in Verisure's stock price.

data.title: Stellantis, Pony.ai to develop self-driving vehicles in Europe
data.description:
(Reuters) -Stellantis and Chinese autonomous driving firm Pony.ai said on Friday that they had signed an agreement to jointly develop and test self-driving vehicles in Europe.

The partnership will combine Pony.ai's autonomous driving software with Stellantis' battery-electric medium-sized van platform designed for SAE Level 4, or hands-off, eyes-off, driving.

Pony.ai, founded in 2016 in China and operating fully driverless robotaxi services in major Chinese cities, will manage European operations through its Luxembourg-based division.

Testing will start with the Peugeot e-Traveller model in Luxembourg, with a rollout across European cities from 2026, focusing on safety, performance, and regulatory compliance, the companies said.

The partnership will initially focus on light commercial vehicles (LCVs), a segment where Stellantis, through its Pro One commercial vehicle business unit, holds a leading position in Europe.

(Reporting by Laura Contemori and Giulio Piovaccari; Editing by Matt Scuffham)

data.title: Fed Forced to Find Its Way in the Dark
data.description:
Fed Forced to Find Its Way in the Dark; Waller Says Policy Path Beyond October Is Uncertain By Michael Maloney

An extended government shutdown is raising the prospect that Federal Reserve officials will make their next interest-rate decision without key economic data that could reconcile a debate over how far and fast to cut rates. The absence of new government data essentially locks in another quarter-point cut at the Fed's next meeting in two weeks following a similar reduction last month. Fed Gov. Christopher Waller said he backed another rate cut this month, but said the central bank's job has been made more challenging by the federal shutdown, which pushed back the release of September inflation statistics until next week and indefinitely postponed official job-market numbers. And banks turned to the Fed to borrow cash overnight in repo trades for a second straight day, pointing to more tightness in U.S. funding markets.

Top News Shutdown Leaves Fed in Dark on Data as It Weighs Next Rate Move

The data blackout caused by the government shutdown comes at an unusually inconvenient moment because Fed officials are navigating an economy

reshaped by Trump administration policy experiments. Tariffs have raised costs for manufacturers and small businesses, which could push consumer prices up. Trade uncertainty is also leading firms to pull back on hiring to protect their profits. Meanwhile, tighter immigration restrictions may be contributing to slower job gains by crimping labor-force growth.

Fed's Waller Says Policy Path Beyond October Is Uncertain

Fed governor Christopher Waller said apparently strong growth was in tension

with signs of a weak labor market. "Since we don't know which way the data will break on this conflict, we need to move with care" in adjusting policy, to avoid a costly mistake, he said.

Fed's Standing Repo Facility Gets More Use

For a second straight day, banks turned to the Fed to borrow cash overnight in repo trades, evidence that the funding markets at the heart of the dollar-backed financial system are growing tighter. Banks borrowed $8.35 billion from the Fed Thursday at its standing repo facility-activity that until this week has been unusual outside specific calendar dates such as month-end and quarter-end periods. As the Fed has shrunk its balance sheet, reserves have been drained from the banking system. Fed officials have said that the level remains at least somewhat above ample, although recent moves in the repo market have suggested a bit more tension in the financial plumbing. Fed officials will weigh that evidence as they decide when to hold the size of the central bank's balance sheet steady rather than continuing to let it shrink. (Dow Jones Newswires)

BOE Should Show More Caution in Future Rate Cuts, Says Chief Economist

The Bank of England should move at a more cautious pace

in lowering its key interest rate over the coming year in recognition of the "stubbornness" of high inflation, its chief economist said Friday.

Bank of Canada Gov. Macklem Expects 'Soft' Growth in Near Term

Bank of Canada Gov. Tiff Macklem said the country's growth outlook remains "soft," as the damage from President Trump's tariff policy begins to broaden globally, and beyond trade-exposed sectors on the domestic front. Canada's economy shrank 1.6% annualized in the second quarter, due to a steep drop in exports. "We do expect growth in the second half of the year," Macklem told an audience in Washington, " but with weakness in business investment and exports and uncertainty about jobs, growth will likely be soft." The central bank cut its benchmark rate last month, by a quarter point to 2.50%, and its next policy-rate decision is scheduled for the end of the month. Some market watchers and economists say a surprise jump in employment for September may prompt Macklem and other senior officials to stand pat at its Oct. 29 decision. (Dow Jones Newswires)

Economy U.S. Deficit Narrows 2% to $1.775 Trillion for Fiscal Year 2025

The federal government ended fiscal year 2025 with a deficit of $1.775 trillion, down about $41 billion or 2%

from the year before, Treasury Department officials said Thursday. The decrease was fueled in part by record customs duties revenues, which included the Trump administration's heightened tariffs.

The Impossible Politics That Mean the Shutdown Is Set to Drag On

Republicans and Democrats both see a likely path to ending the government shutdown, involving extending enhanced Affordable Care Act healthcare subsidies for a year or longer. But there are a series of reasons why no deal has emerged , even with costs set to surge for more than 20 million Americans.

Oil Prices Drop to the Lowest Level in Nearly Five Years

A growing glut of oil and fear of a global economic slowdown have pushed U.S. crude prices to their lowest point since fuel markets were rebounding

from the Covid crash. Most actively traded U.S. oil futures ended Thursday at $56.99 a barrel, down 2.2% on the day and 19% from a year ago. That marked the lowest price since February 2021. Declines this week bring prices below the depths of the spring selloff when President Trump unleashed a barrage of tariffs and sparked worries of economic turmoil.

India Delicately Counters Trump Claim It Will Drop Russian Oil

Chamber of Commerce Sues Over Trump's $100,000 H-1B Visa Fee

The U.S. Chamber of Commerce sued the Trump administration Thursday over hefty new fees

in the H-1B visa program, joining the legal campaign against the administration's changes to a program used by some of the biggest tech companies in the U.S.

Philadelphia Area Factory Unexpectedly Shrinks

Manufacturing activity in the Philadelphia region unexpectedly contracted this month, hit by lower shipments and weakening employment, a monthly survey said. The Federal Reserve Bank of Philadelphia said Thursday that its business-activity index for October sank to minus 12.8 from a positive reading of 23.2 in September. That marked the lowest reading since April, and well below consensus expectations of a positive reading of 9.5 from economists polled by The Wall Street Journal. A total of 25% firms surveyed reported decreases in general activity this month, up from 17% last month, while 12% reported increases, down sharply from 40% last month. Survey responses were collected from Oct. 6 to Oct. 13, after the start of the federal government shutdown. (Dow Jones Newswires)

The Auto Industry Is Panicking About Another Potential Chip Shortage

The auto industry is digesting a new and potentially damaging supply-chain disruption

from an unlikely source: a small Dutch semiconductor manufacturer with an outsize influence on how cars and trucks are made.

Financial Regulation New Credit Fraud Fears Raise More Worries About Regional Banks

Regional banks came under renewed scrutiny

Thursday after Zions Bancorp said it would take a large loss and revealed accusations of fraud against a set of borrowers who had ties to a number of other lenders in the industry.

The disclosures helped send bank stocks reeling to their worst day since President Trump's tariffs hammered the market in April, evidence of how on edge Wall Street is after the recent high-profile bankruptcies of auto supplier First Brands and auto lender Tricolor.

It wasn't clear how widespread any pain would be from the new allegations, which center on a set of investment funds that are now being sued by several banks.

Trump Organization Expands in India, Where Many of Its Partners Face Accusations

India has become the Trump Organization's biggest foreign market for real-estate projects since the presidential election, with nine projects completed or under development. But the projects stand out for the number of active criminal and regulatory cases

facing the company's partners on other projects.

Forward Guidance Friday (all times ET)

9 a.m.: World Bank and International Monetary Fund meetings plenary session

9:15 a.m.: U.S. September industrial production and capacity utilization

12 p.m.: Bank of England Monetary Policy Committee member Megan Greene speaks at Atlantic Council roundtable

1 p.m.: SEC crypto task force roundtable on financial surveillance and privacy

Saturday

7:30 a.m.: Deutsche Bundesbank President Joachim Nagel and WTO Director General Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala speak at OMFIF event

7:30 a.m.: ECB's Piero Cipollone speaks on panel at Euro50Group meeting in Washington

9 a.m.: ECB's Christine Lagarde speaks on Group of Thirty panel in Washington

Commentary From Sports to AI, America Is Awash in Speculative Fever. Washington Is Egging It On.

Today's artificial-intelligence centered boom (or, if you prefer, bubble) has a lot in common with its predecessors, such as leveraged buyouts in the 1980s, dot-com stocks in the 1990s, and housing in the 2000s: stocks at nosebleed valuations, easy credit for risky borrowers, faddish new financial products, writes WSJ's Greg Ip . But something about it feels different. The speculative fever has spread well beyond the usual stocks, bonds and property. Crypto, a brand new asset class, is now valued at some $4 trillion. Americans bet $150 billion on sports last year, up 24% from 2023, according to the American Gaming Association. Gold, a hedge against bubbles, is looking bubbly itself. Speculation has become woven into today's political, economic and cultural psyche. And official Washington, instead of leaning against the speculation, is egging it on: The Federal Reserve is lowering interest rates, the Trump administration is loosening regulations across the financial system, and President Trump's own family is cashing in.

Basis Points The yield on the 10-year U.S. Treasury note dropped below 4%

(MORE TO FOLLOW) Dow Jones Newswires

10-17-25 0725ET

data.title: VEGOILS-Palm ends lower on weak crude oil, logs weekly fall
Indian developer Sobha's profit jumps on robust demand for premium homes
data.description:
(Reuters) -Indian homebuilder Sobha reported second-quarter profit that more than doubled on Friday, helped by high prices and sustained demand for premium apartments.

The Bengaluru-based real estate company said its consolidated net profit rose to 725.3 million rupees ($8.2 million) from 260.9 million rupees a year ago.

Revenues rose 50.7% to 14.08 billion rupees.

KEY CONTEXT

Residential real estate developers have experienced robust growth this year, with Sobha posting record sales in the June quarter, with cuts in India's consumption tax and interest rates expected to further spur consumer demand.

Sales for the second-quarter rose 61% to 19.03 billion rupees.

While Sobha did not have any launches this quarter, pre-sales from its existing inventory aided.

For the rest of the year, demand for residential apartments is expected to remain healthy, especially for branded and credible developers that offer prime gated projects in metro cities, analysts have said.

Peers Prestige Estates Projects and Brigade Enterprises have yet to report their earnings.
